hypothesis:
Describing the input grid: The input contains one colored polyline-like object in the upper part of the grid and another in the lower part, with empty rows separating them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored object, shift every colored cell one step to the right if that does not move it outside the grid; keep cells that would move out of bounds in place. Apply this independently to each object and leave all background cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty grid with a few colored cells placed in specific rows, and the separator row of 4s marks the start of a repeating sequence.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output preserves the original grid size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect the colors appearing in the nonzero cells, then fill alternating rows with full-width bands using those colors in the order they appear from top to bottom, leaving the intervening rows black.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a top pattern and a bottom pattern separated by blank space, where the bottom pattern is the same size as the top pattern and aligned to the same columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps only the rows and columns occupied by the pattern blocks, so it matches the size of one of the blocks.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the top block and the bottom block. Keep only the cells where the bottom block contains color and the top block contains black; all other cells become black. The surviving cells are recolored using the bottom block’s color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid where each column is uniform top-to-bottom, and the three columns use three different colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 3x3 size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recolor each column by shifting its color according to the column position: the left, middle, and right columns are mapped to the next three colors in a fixed ordered palette, specifically 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, 4→3, 8→9, 6→2, 9→8, 5→1, while preserving the column structure.

Describing the input grid: The input is made of a large grid whose rows and columns repeat with period 4, except that some cells are erased to black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reconstruct the complete periodic grid by copying each row pattern every 4 rows and each column pattern every 4 columns, replacing all black cells with the value determined by that 4-step periodicity.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single connected gray object drawn with color 5 on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every gray cell by one of two colors depending on its row position relative to the object’s topmost occupied row: cells in the upper half of the object become teal (8), and cells in the lower half become red (2), with the split determined by the object’s vertical midpoint.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one horizontal separator line and a set of colored marker cells placed either above or below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Each marker cell is extended into a complete line using the orientation implied by its position relative to the separator row: markers above the separator become full rows of their color, while markers below the separator become full columns of their color. The separator row itself is preserved as a full line of its own color.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more rectangular panels separated by a full line of a distinct color acting as a divider.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size is the same as one panel, not the full input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input at the divider line, then take the panel below it, crop away any outer rows/columns that are entirely background or divider color, and output that cropped panel unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing exactly one 2x2 non-black pattern placed near the center, with one colored cell acting as the “pivot” and the other three cells possibly black or different colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the non-black color that appears in the 2x2 pattern and use it to draw a 3-cell-thick diagonal band across the grid. The band is aligned so that the 2x2 input pattern determines the diagonal’s slope and starting position: if the non-black cells form a main-diagonal arrangement, extend that main diagonal into a thick stripe; if they form an anti-diagonal arrangement, extend that anti-diagonal into a thick stripe. Fill every cell touched by the 3-cell-thick diagonal band with that color, leaving all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 binary patterns of a single nonzero color, separated by a full horizontal divider row of color 4.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input into the 4x4 block above the divider and the 4x4 block below it. Compare the two blocks cell-by-cell: if a cell is nonzero in both blocks or zero in both blocks, output 0; if exactly one of the two blocks has a nonzero cell there, output the nonzero color from the top block if it exists, otherwise use the nonzero color from the bottom block. Then replace every nonzero result with green (3).

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing a few nonzero colored cells of several different colors, with the rest black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero cell at position (r, c), draw that same color on the main diagonal position (r+c, c) if it stays within the grid; equivalently, shift each colored cell downward by its column index while keeping the column fixed. If multiple shifted cells overlap, keep the color from the cell that lands there last in reading order.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 colored patterns of the same color, one above and one below a horizontal separator region of black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored pattern, extend every row of the pattern horizontally from its leftmost colored cell to its rightmost colored cell, keeping the original colored rows and leaving all other rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid where each of the four nonzero rows represents a pair of endpoint colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero row, if the two endpoint colors are the same, fill the entire row with that color; if the two endpoint colors are different, leave the row unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid containing only black (0), teal (8), and red (2) cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 1x1 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the total number of teal cells and the total number of red cells in the input. If teal cells are more numerous than red cells, output [[8]]; otherwise output [[0]].

Describing the input grid: The input contains cyan cells (8) that act as anchors in an empty grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every row or column that contains two or more cyan anchors, replace the cells between consecutive anchors with green (3), producing a straight horizontal or vertical green segment connecting the anchors. Keep all cyan anchor cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing one full horizontal row of a repeated “separator” color and two other rows of arbitrary colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 3x3 size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the row whose three cells are all the same color. In the output, keep that entire row as grey (5) and turn every other cell black (0).

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid containing one colored 1-cell-thick horizontal or slanted pattern made of nonzero cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Shift every nonzero cell one row downward, keeping the same column, and replace the top row with zeros. If a cell would move beyond the bottom edge, discard it.

Describing the input grid: The input is an all-black square grid whose size determines the output.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fill the outer border with green. Then draw a single continuous green path that begins at the top-left corner, runs along the top row to the right edge, then proceeds downward along the right edge, then leftward along the bottom region, and continues inward in a clockwise spiral with one-cell turns, leaving all non-path interior cells black.

Describing the input grid: The grid is divided into regions by a dominant background color, and one region contains a compact filled shape while other matching-color pixels are scattered elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the filled shape’s color onto every cell in the same row as each scattered matching-color pixel, but only across the horizontal span occupied by the filled shape. In effect, each scattered pixel “projects” its color horizontally until it reaches the rectangle’s left or right edge, creating a partial row extension.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x5 grids of 2s and 0s, separated by a horizontal line of 4s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 6x5 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input into the top 5x5 grid and the bottom 5x5 grid. For each cell, output green 3 if exactly one of the two corresponding cells is 2, using the top grid cell and the bottom grid cell in the same position; otherwise output black 0. Then compress the 5x5 comparison result by removing the middle row, producing a 6x5 output.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a tiled arrangement separated by a horizontal bar, where each tile is a 3x3 block that may be empty or filled with one color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each tile position, compare the top tile and the bottom tile in the same column of tiles. If exactly one of the two tiles is non-empty, copy that non-empty tile into both positions. If both are non-empty, swap their colors between the two positions. If both are empty, leave both empty.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a small seed pattern in the top-left corner, with the rest of the grid empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Tile the entire grid by repeating the top-left 2x2 block both horizontally and vertically across the whole canvas, preserving the original nonzero colors in their positions within that 2x2 block.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with one nonzero-colored first column and the rest of the cells empty, except that the first column color identifies the object.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same number of rows and columns as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the first column unchanged. Fill the bottom row with yellow. Place red cells on the anti-diagonal of the remaining rows, starting at the top-right corner and moving one step down-left each row, while leaving all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input has a large empty canvas containing a colored configuration that can be decomposed into a set of occupied cells relative to a local coordinate frame.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid size equals the width and height of the occupied-cell pattern after removing all empty margins.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the non-black cells of the main colored configuration, trim away all-black rows and columns, and then compress the remaining pattern by removing any row or column that contains only background between occupied parts. Preserve the relative arrangement of the colored cells, but output them on the smallest grid that still represents the same connectivity pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input contains 5x5 framed motifs where the interior seed color indicates a fill operation to be applied to the motif.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each motif, fill the top border row and the 3x3 inner square with the seed color, but do not overwrite any cells that are already part of the outer frame’s side borders; the frame’s side borders remain their original color.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several 4x4 hollow/filled square patterns made of color 5, separated by rows or columns of 0s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 4x4 pattern, compare it to the corresponding 4x4 pattern in the other half of the grid (top-left with top-right, and bottom-left with bottom-right). Any cell that is 5 in one pattern but 0 in the paired pattern is changed to 2 in the output; cells that are 5 in both remain 5; cells that are 0 in both remain 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 sparse grid where each colored cell indicates the corner of a larger region.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 20x20 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Double the coordinate system and then expand each nonzero cell into a 4x4 block of its color, so that a cell at input coordinate (r, c) fills output rows 2r through 2r+3 and columns 2c through 2c+3. Empty input cells do not produce any output color.

Describing the input grid: In the input, there are several 3x3 blocks of a single color arranged in different positions, with one grey cell acting as a selector.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the 3x3 block whose colored cells are in the same connected component as the grey cell’s nearest colored cell, then output that block normalized so its colored cells are shifted into a 3x3 canvas and recolored to the block color, preserving the exact black/non-black layout.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 blocks of colored cells placed above and below a horizontal divider row of 4s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the entire grid across the horizontal line through the separator row, but preserve only the colored cells from the reflected positions while leaving existing colored cells unchanged; this produces vertical symmetry with the top block repeated at the bottom and the bottom block repeated at the top.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid whose top rows contain a short vertical stack of nonzero color bands, followed by a large block of black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same number of columns as the input, and its height is the same as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input unchanged, then fill the last two rows by mirroring the first two non-black rows from the top in reverse order: the second-to-last row becomes a copy of the second row, and the last row becomes a copy of the first row.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid partitioned into several solid-color square/rectangular regions, each region using a different color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a single-column grid whose height equals the number of distinct non-background colors present in the input, ordered by their first appearance from top to bottom.
Describing how to transform the grid: Scan the input from top to bottom and record each new color the first time it appears, ignoring repeated occurrences of the same color. Write those colors into the output as one cell per row in that order.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a top 4x4 colored motif and a bottom 4x4 colored motif separated by a horizontal row of 4s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each non-background cell in the top motif, place the same color at the vertically mirrored position below the divider; for each non-background cell in the bottom motif, place the same color at the vertically mirrored position above the divider. This creates a full top-bottom symmetric completion while preserving the original divider row.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more 4x4 blocks of non-background cells separated by a full horizontal divider row of 4s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the 4x4 blocks.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input at the row of 4s into two 4x4 grids. Compare them cell-by-cell: if a cell is the same in both grids, put 0 in the output; if the cells differ, put 3 in the output.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of colored rectangular regions whose border cells define the shape, with black background separating them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each rectangle, identify its interior by eroding the rectangle by one cell from all sides. Then fill that eroded interior with teal (8). Rectangles with no interior after erosion remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more colored 2x3 or 2x4 “frame” shapes made of a nonzero color, with empty space elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every nonzero cell in the input, keep it unchanged unless it belongs to a fully enclosed 2x2 interior of a frame; for each such enclosed interior cell, replace it with yellow (4). All other cells remain the same.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more small colored 2x2 blocks placed on a 10x10 background of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored 2x2 block, draw a diagonal trail of the same color starting from the block and extending away from the other colored block(s) in the grid. The trail is placed along the main diagonal direction toward the nearest empty corner, and it stops when it reaches the grid edge or another colored block.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a sparse 23x23 grid with several isolated colored pixels arranged so that their row/column positions matter.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored pixel in the input, copy its color into the entire row and entire column that contain it, and then combine all such filled rows and columns into the output, keeping background cells black where no copied row or column passes.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two long divider lines: one horizontal and one vertical, each made of a single color, and the non-divider cells contain a pattern in the four quadrants.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size equals the size of the bottom-right quadrant plus one row and one column for the divider lines.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the bottom-right quadrant defined by the intersection of the horizontal and vertical divider lines. Then build the output by placing the horizontal divider color as the first row, the vertical divider color as the first column, and filling the remaining cells with the bottom-right quadrant contents. Cells in the interior that are not part of the extracted quadrant remain black unless occupied by the copied pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two separate 3x3 blocks of yellow cells placed in different locations on a 9x9 canvas, with black cells elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 yellow block, examine its pattern of yellow and black cells. Any black cell that is orthogonally adjacent to at least two yellow cells within that same 3x3 block becomes orange; all yellow cells remain yellow and all other cells stay black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty square grid with one or more nonzero cells, usually a single colored cell acting as a seed.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the nonzero seed cell and fill the entire grid with a diagonal mirror pattern centered on that cell: every cell at the same Chebyshev distance from the seed along the two diagonals receives the seed color, creating an X-shaped symmetry pattern. If there are multiple seeds, use the unique nonzero cell of the input as the center of the generated pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x10 grid with a 4x4 separator made of color 5, splitting the grid into four 4x4 quadrants that contain colored shapes on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same 10x10 size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Leave the top-left, top-right, and bottom-left quadrants unchanged. In the bottom-right quadrant, replace every non-black cell by 5 while keeping black cells as 0. The separator line of 5s stays unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 3x3 regions of colored cells separated by horizontal divider lines, and the task is to identify where the two regions differ.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input into the top 3x3 region and the bottom 3x3 region. For each cell position, output 1 only when the top cell and bottom cell are different in color status and the top cell is colored; otherwise output 0.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains a single colored cross-like structure: one full horizontal or vertical line of a nonzero color, with another orthogonal line or partial pattern intersecting it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the intersection cell of the main colored lines. Expand that intersection into a 3x3 yellow block centered on the intersection, but only write yellow into cells that are currently black. Keep all original nonzero cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 20x20 grid split into four 5x20 horizontal bands, with the first band and third band using one nonzero color and the second band and fourth band using another nonzero color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same 20x20 size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the 4x4 subpattern formed by taking only the non-background cells from the first 5-row band with the 4x4 subpattern from the third 5-row band, and likewise compare the second and fourth bands. Wherever the two corresponding subpatterns differ, replace the differing cells in the lower band with green; otherwise leave the cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid filled with two non-black colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is also a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the color that occupies the top row of the input. Output 5 in every cell whose color is the same as the top-row color, and output 0 in every other cell.

Describing the input grid: The input contains four sparse patterns, each occupying a distinct quadrant, and each pattern uses one dominant color plus occasional accent colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the entire arrangement across both the horizontal and vertical center lines, but only for the nonzero cells: every colored cell is moved to the position obtained by rotating the grid 180 degrees around the center, while background cells remain background.

Describing the input grid: The input is composed of two equal-size square blocks above and below a divider row of 4s. The top block and bottom block share the same nonzero color layout except that the bottom block has missing cells marked 0.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the top block into the bottom block cell-by-cell, but only where the bottom block currently has 0; leave any nonzero bottom-block cells unchanged. The top block is not modified.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one non-black rectangular pattern, usually embedded in a larger black canvas. The pattern is made of two colors: a background color and a second color marking a small motif inside it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the tight bounding box around the non-black pattern, with all surrounding black rows and columns removed.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the input to the smallest rectangle that contains all non-black cells, keeping the original cell colors unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several isolated objects in a large grid, where some objects are blue, some are red, and some are grey; the relevant objects are the ones that appear as a pair across different quadrants.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare objects of the same geometry across the grid. If an object has an equivalent object elsewhere in the input with a different color, change the object to green in the output. If no differently colored equivalent exists, leave the object unchanged. All object shapes and locations stay fixed.

Describing the input grid: The input contains four square panels separated by a full row of zeros: the top-left panel uses one color, the top-right panel uses another, the bottom-left panel uses a third, and the bottom-right panel uses a fourth.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 2x2 when the input has 4x4 panels, 3x2 when the input has 5x5/6x6 panels, and generally one cell per panel.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each panel, check whether the panel is symmetric under a 180-degree rotation. If it is symmetric, place the panel’s color in the output cell; otherwise place 0. The output is arranged in the same 2x2 / 3x2 layout as the four panels.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing blue cells and black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is also a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every blue cell with red. Then, for each column, if that column contains at least one blue cell in the input, fill every cell in that same column with red in the output; otherwise leave only the replaced red cells.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several 8-connected line drawings made of one color, separated by a full horizontal divider line of another color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each connected drawing above and below the divider. For every cell that belongs to any drawing, recolor it by comparing the corresponding cell positions in the two halves: if the upper and lower drawings both have a colored cell at that position, keep the original color; if only one half has a colored cell there, recolor it green. Leave background cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one colored object made of nonzero cells on an otherwise black square grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the nonzero cells in the input and keep them in place, but recolor every nonzero cell to green.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square 3x3 grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x6.
Describing how to transform the grid: Form the output by tiling four transformed versions of the input: top-left is the original, top-right is rotated 90 degrees clockwise, bottom-left is rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise, and bottom-right is rotated 180 degrees. The four quadrants are assembled in that order into a 2x2 block.

Describing the input grid: In the input, there is a collection of same-colored rectangular blocks arranged so that each block corresponds to one cell of a larger 3x3 symbolic grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the bounding arrangement of the nine blocks, then downsample the whole structure by a factor of 2 in both dimensions using nearest-neighbor-style block sampling: every 2x2 area in the input becomes one cell in the output, with nonzero cells becoming the block color and zero cells staying zero. This produces a 9x9 image that preserves the arrangement of the original blocks.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains a single colored pattern in the upper-left or upper-right area and one isolated gray marker elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the colored pattern nearest to the gray marker, then duplicate that entire pattern so that its top-left cell is placed at the cell directly below the marker and one column to the left, leaving the original pattern in place.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single horizontal line of a nonzero color, and above it there is a sparse pattern made of that same color on an otherwise black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same width as the input, and its height equals the height of the region above the horizontal line.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the rows above the horizontal line, then repeat that extracted block downward enough times to fill the output height, preserving the exact row order and colors.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing a small colored pattern in the upper-left 3x3 area, with the rest mostly empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the 3x3 colored pattern into every 3x3 block of the 9x9 grid, using the same relative position inside each block; cells that are empty in the original 3x3 pattern remain empty in the copies.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more 2x2 grey blocks placed on a large black canvas.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey 2x2 block, place four 1-cell markers around it at the four diagonal corner positions of the 4x4 region centered on that block: top-left gets blue (1), top-right gets red (2), bottom-left gets green (3), and bottom-right gets yellow (4). Leave the original grey block unchanged and do this for every grey block independently.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of several candidate motifs, each one a mostly uniform colored block with a few internal cells that differ in color or are missing.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size matches the bounding box size of the selected motif.
Describing how to transform the grid: Among all nonzero connected motifs, choose the one whose bounding box is largest in area. Extract its bounding box, then fill all empty cells inside that box with the motif’s primary color so that the object becomes a solid block, while retaining any internal accent colors that were already present. The transformed motif is the output.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 grids separated by a full row of 4s, with the top grid in blue/black and the bottom grid in red/black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output by marking 3 in every cell where exactly one of the two input grids has a nonzero value, and 0 where both grids are either nonzero or both zero.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more colored seed cells placed in an otherwise black grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored seed cell, draw an L-shape anchored at that seed: fill all cells to the right in the same row from the seed to the last column, and fill all cells below in the same column from the seed to the last row. All filled cells use the seed’s color, and black cells remain unchanged unless covered by one of these L-shapes.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large background of 1s containing a smaller non-1 rectangular region made of other colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size is the bounding box of the non-1 region.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the smallest rectangle that contains all cells whose value is not 1, and then replace every 1 inside that extracted rectangle with 0 while keeping the other colors unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a rectangular frame made of color 5, with a smaller interior region marked by color 8 on a background of 0.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the bounding box of the color-8 region inside the frame, then compress that region into a 3x3 output by mapping its occupied cells to the corresponding cells of the 3x3 grid and leaving all other cells 0. If multiple input cells map to the same output cell, the result is 8.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several colored objects, each object using one nonzero color and separated from the others by black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is a square whose side length is the input height multiplied by the input width.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each nonzero cell in the input by a copy of the entire input grid, scaled into a block of size height x width, and place that block at the position corresponding to the cell’s row and column in the output. In the copied block, keep only the cells of the copied input that have the same color as the triggering cell; all other cells become black.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of several 3-filled shapes with one embedded 2 in each shape, separated by empty space and a divider row of 4s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps the same grid size.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each shape, propagate the 2 along the entire connected component of 3s in the same row direction and the same column direction from the 2’s position, turning the whole cross through the shape into a filled pattern where the row arm becomes 2s and the column arm stays 3s, while all untouched cells remain as in the input.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more 4x4 blocks of the same nonzero color, separated by rows/columns of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each 4x4 nonzero block with a new 4x4 pattern derived from the block’s position: if the block is in the upper-left or lower-right quadrant, fill its 4x4 area with a fixed “frame” pattern using colors 1, 4, and 2; if the block is in the other quadrant, use the same frame pattern translated to that block’s location. Zeros outside the blocks remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two 8xN or similarly sized panels stacked vertically with a separator row of 4s. The top panel is mostly a template of 0s and 8s, and the bottom panel contains a small colored seed pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same shape as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the colored seed pattern from the bottom panel as a stencil and tile it across the positions of the 8-cells in both panels according to the 0/8 layout of each panel, converting matching 8 positions into the seed colors and leaving 0 positions unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a grid whose bottom rows include a nonzero “base” pattern, and the rows above it are mostly empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the nonzero pattern from the bottom two rows upward into the empty area by reflecting it across the horizontal line just above the base. When a copied cell lands in an empty place, write the copied color there; keep existing nonzero cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 5x7 grid containing a 3x3 pattern embedded in the left and right sides, with two empty rows in the middle.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the 3x3 region formed by the first, second, and last columns and by the first, second, and last rows. Fill each output cell with the color found at the matching position in this extracted pattern, using the nonzero cell if one of the paired positions is colored and the other is black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more hollow 5-colored rectangles, each acting as a container around an empty interior.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect each hollow rectangle and fill its interior with a color chosen by the rectangle’s interior size: interiors of size 1 are colored fuchsia (6), interiors of size 2x2 are colored orange (7), and interiors of size 3x3 are colored teal (8). The border cells remain 5.

Describing the input grid: The input is split into two halves by a full horizontal background row. Each half is a pattern of one foreground color plus background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the top half’s foreground/background pattern onto the bottom half by comparing cells position-by-position. In the output, mark the separator row with the foreground color, and in the top half change any cell that does not match the corresponding cell in the bottom half to color 3. Keep all matching cells unchanged and keep the entire bottom half unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains only the colors 1, 7, and 8, with 7 appearing as the color to be modified.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every cell whose value is 7 with 5, and leave every other cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a tiled mosaic made of repeated 5x5 pattern blocks separated by full rows/columns of a special separator color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a single 7x7 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the 5x5 repeating block pattern in the input, then extract the 5x5 “summary” of which block-types occur in the mosaic and convert it into a 7x7 frame by surrounding the extracted interior with the separator color. The interior cells are colored by the dominant non-separator color of the corresponding block position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a set of vertical stripes or blocks where each colored cell type is paired with background cells and often overlaid on color 5 regions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, identify the leftmost non-5 nonzero color present in that row and repaint all nonzero cells in that row with that color, leaving zeros unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing several isolated grey cells placed in some rows and columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each grey cell with a 3x3 block of blue cells whose top-left corner is aligned to the grey cell’s position rounded down to the nearest multiple of 3 in both row and column. All other cells remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid with a few colored anchor cells placed at different positions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each nonzero cell as a seed and replace every cell whose row index is greater than or equal to the seed's row and whose column index is greater than or equal to the seed's column with that seed's color, using the color from the seed that is closest in Manhattan distance to the cell; this yields a bottom-right dominance map of the original seeds.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more equal-sized square blocks separated by a full row of a constant delimiter color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the grid into the square blocks above and below the delimiter row. Compare corresponding cells in the two blocks: if the two cells are the same color, keep the cell’s original color in the output; if they are different, replace that cell with green (3). Keep the delimiter row unchanged if it is present in the output region.

Describing the input grid: The input is a sparse grid with one colored seed cell near the top-left and one colored seed cell near the bottom-left, separated by empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Expand each seed into a color-specific diagonal stripe pattern across the grid by repeatedly placing the seed color at positions whose column parity matches the row distance from that seed. The upper seed expands downward and rightward to form a repeated wedge of that color, and the lower seed expands upward and rightward to form a repeated wedge of the second color. Where a row is not reached by a seed’s wedge, it stays black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains several colors, but one color is repeated as multiple separated instances while another color forms a small anchor shape.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same shape as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Choose the color that appears in the most cells as the template color. Then draw that color along the row and column lines connecting every occurrence of that color, but only where the path intersects cells that are either black or already part of the same template color. Other colors act as obstacles and are preserved.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 5-row grid where the top 3 rows are empty, and the bottom 2 rows contain a repeated motif made of colored cells and black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the existing pattern in the bottom 2 rows horizontally to the right until the pattern fills the entire row width, repeating the motif exactly as it appears from left to right.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x6 grid containing two 3x3 binary masks, one on the left and one on the right, separated only by different colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the grid into left and right 3x3 blocks. Compute the exclusive-or of the two blocks cell by cell: output 6 where the cells differ in emptiness (one is 0 and the other is nonzero), and output 0 where they match (both zero or both nonzero).

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x10 canvas with a few single colored marker cells in the top row, a blank separator row, and then several 2x2 or 3x4 rectangular blocks made of one repeated nonzero color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each connected nonzero rectangular block below the separator, replace every cell of that block with the color of the nearest marker cell in the top row when measured by column alignment: blocks in the left half take the left marker color, blocks in the middle take the middle marker color, and blocks in the right half take the right marker color; the top marker row and separator row remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one sparse pattern of colored cells embedded in black space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the smallest rectangle that contains all nonzero cells, then tile that rectangle periodically over the whole grid in both directions. When tiles overlap, later placements overwrite earlier ones, producing a full-grid repetition of the original local pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x9 grid split into two 4x4 colored patterns by a vertical separator column of 5s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 10x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep only the left 4 columns of the input. Replace every nonzero cell in that 4x4 block with the color of the nonzero cell in the corresponding position of the right 4x4 block, while leaving zero cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two or more separated rectangular panels of colored cells, with a solid separator row of a different color between them and a large background color filling the unused space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the size of one panel, i.e. the bounding rectangle of the non-background pattern in a single panel.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract each colored panel from the input, ignore the separator row and background, and place the panels into the output by merging them cell-by-cell: keep the panel color where a panel has a non-background value, and use the background color where all panels have background. If multiple panels overlap at a cell, the non-background value from the corresponding panel is preserved in its own region, producing a composite of the visible panel fragments.

Describing the input grid: The input is divided into two or more subgrids by full lines of 5s, and each subgrid is a binary pattern made of 0s and 5s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare each subgrid to the subgrid directly opposite it across the separator line; any cell that is 0 in one subgrid and 5 in the opposite subgrid is recolored to 4 in the output, while cells that match remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a 10x10 background of 5s with 0s marking a set of local patterns that need to be classified.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid stays 10x10.
Describing how to transform the grid: First identify each maximal connected component of 0s. Then color the component by its bounding-box dimensions: components with bounding box 2x2 become 1, components with bounding box 1x2 or 2x1 become 2, and components with bounding box 1x1 become 3; keep the 5s as background.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x11 patterns separated by a row of 5s. The top pattern and bottom pattern are complementary masks over the same 5x11 area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has size 5x11.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the bottom pattern onto the top pattern by shifting the bottom pattern upward by 5 rows and combining them cellwise. In the combined result, preserve all colored cells from both halves, but when a cell is colored in both halves, keep the color from the lower half.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more colored figures separated by a solid horizontal divider line in a different color, with empty background elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all cells above the divider unchanged. For the empty cells below the divider, trace the silhouette of the top figure by taking the top figure’s non-background cells and projecting their positions into the lower empty region, using the same relative coordinates but mirrored vertically with respect to the divider’s bounding area; fill those projected cells with the color of the top figure’s outline/object, leaving all existing non-background cells below the divider unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing one background color and one foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 3x3 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Rotate the entire grid 90 degrees counterclockwise.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a few anchor cells of one color and a few fixed obstacle/marker cells of another color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the anchor cells to infer a 4-cell-wide periodic lattice: copy the anchor arrangement into every 4-row by 4-column block of the grid, but only into locations that are empty in the input; do not overwrite any existing nonzero cells.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single polyomino of 4s on a 9x9 black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size is determined by the span of the object after applying a fixed 2x2 replication.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each 4-cell in the input with a 2x2 block of 4s, preserving the input's relative arrangement. Then crop away all-black rows and columns from the result to produce the output grid.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a blank upper half and a filled lower half, separated implicitly by the midpoint of the grid height.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the lower half vertically into the upper half, so the top half becomes a vertical mirror copy of the bottom half while the bottom half stays unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid containing some nonzero colored cells and many zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has twice the number of rows and twice the number of columns as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output by tiling the input into a 2x2 arrangement, but replace every 0 in the repeated copies with 8 while keeping all nonzero cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is an \(N \times N\) grid containing two non-background colors, one of which appears in a repeating diagonal/positional pattern and the other acts as filler.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every cell that is not equal to the most frequent non-background color with 0, and recolor every cell equal to that most frequent color using the other non-background color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid made of repeated 2x2 colored blocks separated by rows and columns of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x1 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each non-zero color, count the total number of times it appears in the 2x2 blocks. Then take the three colors with the smallest positive counts, ordered from most frequent to least frequent among those three, and write them top to bottom in the output.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two 3x3 binary masks stacked vertically with a separator row: the upper mask is encoded by 9s and the lower mask is encoded by 1s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a single 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: First convert both masks to boolean grids, then compute their logical difference in both directions and combine it: mark 2 in any cell where the top mask and bottom mask disagree, but only if the top mask has 9 in that cell and the bottom has 0, or the bottom has 1 and the top has 0; otherwise mark 0. This is equivalent to highlighting cells occupied in exactly one of the two masks.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x10 grid with several 2x2 colored blocks placed in different regions, separated by empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find each 2x2 block of nonzero cells in the input. If the block is made of four cells that are all the same color, leave it unchanged; otherwise replace that entire 2x2 block with green (3) in the same location.

Describing the input grid: The input has three 5x5 panels stacked vertically, with the top and bottom panels being mirror-related versions of a pattern and the middle panel acting as a filler color source.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 5x5 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each cell position, if the top and bottom panels differ, copy the middle panel's color into the output at that position; if they match, put black in the output.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two similar 1-cell-thick diagonal/zigzag line shapes of different colors, one in the upper part and one in the lower part, separated by empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps the same overall grid size, but only the upper shape is redrawn in place while the lower area becomes empty.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the shape that is positioned higher in the grid fixed, and shift its leftmost/topmost occupied part one step left whenever that move is possible without changing the shape’s connectivity; do not move the lower shape, and erase it from the output.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more isolated colored cells on a blank grid, with a prominent divider row that marks the board into regions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored cell, fill the entire row at that cell’s row index with the same color, but only at columns that are congruent to the seed’s column modulo a spacing derived from the board width and the seed position. If multiple seeds exist, their generated horizontal stripes are all drawn into the output, producing alternating repeated color bands separated by blank cells.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of repeated pattern tiles separated by blank bands of 0s; one tile is the “answer” tile because it uses a different palette color than the other tiles.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is exactly the size of that special tile.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the tile whose nonzero cells all share the unique nonzero color in the input, then crop that tile and replace all of its nonzero values with 3, preserving the arrangement of 0s and nonzeros.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid of single-digit colors, and every row is identical to every other row.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each nonzero color c by a new color obtained from a fixed color permutation: 1→4, 2→6, 3→4, 4→3, 5→1, 6→2, 8→9, 9→8, and keep the row/column structure unchanged. Apply this mapping independently to every cell.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large rectangular carpet made of a repeating tiled motif, with some cells replaced by 0 as missing holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps the same overall grid size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recover the carpet by extending the repeating tile pattern across the entire grid. Determine the smallest repeating block from the nonzero regions, then fill every 0 cell with the value that would appear there by continuing the same horizontal and vertical repetition.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single pattern of color 5 that is composed of two interlocking block regions separated by empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the maximal horizontal and vertical stretches of color-5 cells that form the visible blocks. Recolor the cells in the upper-left / larger block region with teal (8), and recolor the cells in the remaining block region with red (2). Preserve the exact geometry of the original 5-cells and keep all background cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a sparse grid with a few colored “seed” cells, and the same colors may appear in multiple rows and columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero cell in the input, extend its color across the entire row and the entire column that pass through that cell, but only through cells that are still empty. If multiple extensions intersect, keep the color of the cell that is encountered first by a consistent priority order based on the original seed positions.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more same-colored rectangular frames or bars, with one color acting as a separator and the remaining regions containing mixed colors and background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is formed by taking the interior regions between the rectangular separators, so its size is the height of the area between the separators and the width of the area between the separators.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the unique solid rectangle/bar color that spans an entire row or column and use it as a divider. Remove that divider row/column and extract the rectangular region(s) adjacent to it that contain the non-divider pattern. Keep only the cells that are non-background inside those regions, and map every non-background cell to green in the output while preserving background as black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 2x2 block where one cell is red and the other cell(s) are a target color, embedded in an empty square grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the red cell as an orientation marker: the target color is copied outward along the diagonal opposite the red cell, creating a staircase-like triangular expansion. Each next diagonal step adds one more cell of the target color, producing a growing triangular wedge that continues until it touches the grid boundary.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 30x30 grid containing two sparse 4x4 color-occupancy patterns made from one active color and background 0, separated by a horizontal row of 4s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 4x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the 4x4 region whose nonzero cells form the more compact clustered object, meaning the active color cells are connected and occupy the same relative positions as the output. Copy that region’s nonzero pattern into a 4x4 output grid, replacing its active color with 3 and leaving background cells as 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains pairs of same-colored cells positioned so that their relative arrangement suggests a line to be completed.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color, find the two extreme cells of that color and draw the straight 8-directional connection between them using that color, then preserve any existing colored cells; the result is the union of the original cells and the connecting line.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 cups of the same color, one in the upper half and one in the lower half, with the same shape mirrored vertically around the separating blank row.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each cup independently, identify the highest row containing colored cells and then “pour water” downward by copying the colored cells of each row to all cells between the leftmost and rightmost colored positions on that row, creating a filled-in version of the cup while keeping the cup’s outer footprint unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing four colored endpoint cells arranged as two pairs, with the rest mostly black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the two nonzero cells in each row that form a pair. If the two cells have the same color, fill every cell between them in that row with the same color; otherwise leave the row unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid made of black cells and two colored cell types.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 1x1 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count all cells of each color in the input. Output the color that has the larger total cell count; if the two colors have the same count, output black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains teal 8s arranged in pairs or singletons across rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the topmost row and bottommost row that contain teal 8s. In each such row, if there are two teal 8s, fill the cells between them with green. If there is only one teal 8 in a row, extend a vertical green line downward from that 8 to the next row containing a teal 8 in the same column, stopping before the lower 8.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid with three horizontal bands, and each band should be tested for whether it is uniform.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if the row contains at least two different colors, turn that row black (0); if the row is completely uniform, turn that row gray (5).

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid containing one connected non-black pattern made of a single color, positioned somewhere in the grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Move the entire colored pattern down by exactly one row, keeping its shape, color, and column positions unchanged; cells that move outside the grid disappear and their old locations become black.

Describing the input grid: The input is an empty square grid of 0s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fill the grid with 3s according to an inward spiral that alternates between drawing the outer perimeter and then every second cell on the next inner turns, starting from the top-left corner and proceeding clockwise. The spiral stops when it reaches the central area, leaving a 0-valued cavity whose size and shape depend on the grid dimensions.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large grid with a uniform background color, plus several small monochrome “marker” cells and one or more larger solid rectangular blocks of a different color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each large rectangular block, look at the row and column positions of the isolated marker cells of the same color as that block. Fill the entire row segments and column segments of the block so that every cell lying in the same row or column as a marker and intersecting the block becomes the block’s color, leaving all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x5 patterns separated by a horizontal divider row of 4s, and both patterns use the same foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x5.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top and bottom 5x5 patterns cell by cell. Mark a cell green if exactly one of the two patterns has the foreground color there, and black if both have the same value. The output is the 6x5 cellwise difference grid formed by this comparison, using the first five rows from the comparison and a sixth row from the remaining unmatched part of the lower pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large grid divided into equal square cells by a full-line separator color. Each cell contains either background or a colored “petal” pattern, and some cells are more complete than others.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the cell pattern that is the most complete among all repeated cells (the one with the fewest missing colored pixels, using the non-background color inside the cells). Then copy that complete cell pattern into every other cell, preserving the separator lines and background layout, so the whole grid becomes a repetition of the most complete flower petal.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with a single top-row pattern of colored cells and empty space below.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Generate the output by vertically repeating a 2-row motif derived from the first row: row 0 is the input’s first row, row 1 is the first row shifted one cell left with wraparound disabled and each original colored cell duplicated diagonally down-left; then repeat these two rows downward until the grid is filled.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single solid-colored vertical block in the first column, with all other cells black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the original first column unchanged. Place a red 2 in the rightmost cell of the top row, then continue placing 2s one step down-left on each subsequent row, forming a descending diagonal. The bottom row is then filled entirely with yellow 4s.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a frame-like arrangement of a single color around a sparse pattern, where the relevant information is the internal pattern’s occupied extent.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the minimal rectangle that contains all non-background cells of the interior pattern.
Describing how to transform the grid: Remove all rows and columns that contain only background cells outside the interior pattern, keeping only the tight crop of the non-background interior cells.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 4x4 framed region with a marker inside it, and sometimes a second framed region below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: First, identify the framed region whose interior contains a nonzero marker. Then replace the entire top edge of that frame with the marker’s color and flood-fill the interior of the frame with that same color, while leaving the frame border and any other separate framed regions unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5-colored shapes of the same size, one above and one below a separator row/area, and each shape acts like a template for the other.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the two shapes cell-by-cell within their aligned bounding boxes. Wherever exactly one of the two shapes has a 5 and the other has a 0, mark that position in the output with red; wherever both have 5 or both have 0, leave the cell unchanged. Apply this comparison independently to both shapes’ locations so the red cells appear only in the differing interior positions.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a 10x10 board split by a solid yellow horizontal separator into two 4x4 sparse boards: one in the top half and one in the bottom half. Each board contains a few colored single cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 20x20 grid, made by expanding the 10x10 input so that each input cell becomes a 2x2 block.
Describing how to transform the grid: First enlarge every cell into a 2x2 block, so the whole board doubles in both dimensions. Then, for each non-yellow sparse board, fill each 2x2 block using the color of the corresponding single cell from the original board. The yellow separator becomes an empty 2x2 empty band, and all empty cells stay empty after expansion.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a gray marker cell that separates the relevant pattern from the rest of the grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the 3x3 neighborhood centered on the gray marker’s row and column, then take the colored cells from that neighborhood and map them into the output by preserving their relative positions; any cell in that neighborhood that is not colored becomes 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several colored corner-like patterns placed in different regions, with a solid colored separator line between them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each nonzero pattern block and copy it into the opposite corner by rotating the block 180 degrees around its own center, so that the missing symmetric counterpart is completed. Keep the separator line and any already-existing blocks unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid where the top part contains a vertical sequence of colored rows, followed by a block of empty rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the non-empty rows from the top of the grid to the bottom in reverse order, preserving each row exactly, so the grid becomes vertically symmetric around its middle.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mosaic of square tiles, each tile containing one main color with possible internal noise from other colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has one cell for each tile position in the mosaic, so its dimensions equal the number of tiles vertically and horizontally.
Describing how to transform the grid: Partition the grid into equal square tiles. For each tile, take the color appearing in the tile’s top-left corner as the tile’s label, and place those labels into the output in the same tile layout order.

Describing the input grid: The input is divided into four equal quadrants by the blank middle area, with one colored motif appearing in one quadrant and another colored motif appearing in the diagonally opposite quadrant.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Complete the 4-corner symmetry by taking each motif and placing its horizontal mirror, vertical mirror, and 180-degree rotation in the remaining three quadrants. The motifs are not recolored; only their positions are mirrored.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with one symmetric object formed by color 2, interrupted by zeros at the locations where the cross is incomplete.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same shape as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the existing color-2 cells to infer the full cross by symmetry and continuity, then draw the missing parts of that cross in color 8 while keeping the original color-2 cells and all non-cross cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two colored rectangles, one in the upper half and one in the lower half, each made of a single color with empty interior space or solid fill.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored rectangle, find its bounding box and recolor every cell strictly inside that box to 8 if it is not already part of the rectangle’s border. The outermost cells of the rectangle remain their original color, and all other cells remain 0.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of one or more colored pattern objects arranged in rows, each with a distinctive hollow-center top row and a lower row that defines a firing direction.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Preserve the original objects, then project a single yellow cell from each object along the line extending from its hollow center. The projected yellow cell is placed at the same relative offset from the object as if the object were emitting a bullet straight outward from its opening, and only the landing cell is marked.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two non-overlapping 2x2 colored blocks placed on a 10x10 black canvas.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the 2x2 block that is closer to the top-left corner and the 2x2 block that is farther away. Starting from the top-left cell of the first block, draw a 1-cell-wide diagonal line of that block’s color stepping one cell down-right until it reaches the second block, then continue the same diagonal line through the second block.

Describing the input grid: The input has a set of scattered colored pixels and a horizontal separator line; the scattered pixels indicate key control points.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the colored pixels as anchors of a symmetric frame centered at the midpoint of the grid. Project each anchor outward along its row and column to form continuous line segments, and union all such projections to create a connected frame. Recolor all resulting frame cells with the anchor color, and leave all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several colored separator lines that divide the grid into rectangular regions: a middle horizontal frame line and one or more vertical frame lines. Inside the framed area are sparse colored points on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the rectangular region formed by taking the cells between the main frame lines, excluding the outer background margins.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the subgrid bounded by the same-colored frame lines. Keep the frame colors as the border of the output. For every cell inside the frame, replace it with the color of the corresponding point if that point lies in the same relative position in the framed region; otherwise leave it black. This effectively crops to the framed panel and preserves the marked points within it.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more small 3x3 shapes made of color 4, separated by empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 shape, fill every empty cell inside its 3x3 bounding box with color 7, leaving all existing 4-cells unchanged. This completes each shape into a solid 3x3 square or a 3x3 pattern where 7 marks the missing cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty square grid with a single non-zero cell marking a source point.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the source color into every cell whose position lies on one of the two diagonals through the source point, extending outward in all four diagonal directions until the edge of the grid is reached; all other cells remain 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one gray frame and two shapes associated by position: one shape lies in the upper-left area inside/adjacent to the frame, and another matching shape lies in the lower-right area outside the frame.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect the shape outside the frame and recolor it to gray, but only when its pattern matches the shape inside the frame. All other objects remain unchanged. The gray frame stays gray.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a 3×3 matrix of equally sized subgrids, separated by 8-colored lines. Each subgrid may contain several pink blocks placed at different positions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3×3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each subgrid, count the pink blocks and also note the subgrid’s position in the 3×3 layout. Output blue (1) exactly for those regions where the pink count matches the region index rule: the top-left to bottom-right diagonal regions are blue when they contain at least one pink block, while all off-diagonal regions are blue only when they contain exactly two pink blocks; otherwise output black (0).
