Describing the input grid: The input contains two colored lines crossing at one point, with empty cells around them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the intersection of the vertical and horizontal lines, then paint every background cell in the 3x3 neighborhood around the intersection yellow, leaving all non-background cells in their original colors.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 20x20 grid containing four 5x5 subgrids arranged in a 2x2 layout, with one subgrid acting as the reference cube face pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 20x20 size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the unique 5x5 subgrid whose pattern is most complete or most irregularly distinctive, and replace the other three 5x5 subgrids with copies of that same 5x5 pattern. The copied patterns must be aligned to the same cell positions as the source subgrid, so the result is a tiled repetition of the found cube face.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid whose colors encode a count through the diversity of colors present.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Let k be the number of distinct colors in the input. Create an output grid of black cells, then place gray cells in a fixed pattern determined by k: k=1 means fill the top row, k=2 means fill the main diagonal, and k=3 means fill the anti-diagonal.

Describing the input grid: The input contains multiple 3-cell patterns of the same shape, separated into regions above and below the central horizontal band.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the unique 3-cell pattern closest to the center and use it as a stencil. For every other occurrence of a 3-cell pattern in the grid, replace its color with the center pattern’s color, leaving all background cells unchanged and keeping the pattern’s geometry and position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 pattern blocks of the same layout, one above and one below a horizontal divider row. The two blocks use different colors, but the positions of colored cells and empty cells follow the same geometric pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same width and the same height as one of the 4x4 blocks, so it is a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the top 4x4 block and the bottom 4x4 block. Compare them cell by cell after treating all nonzero colors as equivalent to "filled". If a cell is filled in exactly one of the two blocks, color that output cell green; if both blocks agree on filled/empty, leave it black. Ignore the specific colors in the input; the output is the XOR of the two block patterns.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one non-black rectangular pattern embedded in a larger black canvas. The pattern is made of a uniform background color with a smaller contrasting-color shape inside it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the bounding box of the non-black pattern, with the same height and width as that rectangle.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the smallest rectangle containing all non-black cells, then flip that rectangle vertically and horizontally (equivalently rotate it 180 degrees), and output the transformed rectangle.

Describing the input grid: The gray frame defines a reference tile made of a 4x4 shape and a single non-gray color, while other isolated 4x4 tiles may appear in different colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output preserves the original grid size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the unique 4x4 tile enclosed by the gray frame and treat its interior pattern as a stamp. For every other 4x4 tile in the grid, if its shape matches the framed tile’s interior pattern, recolor that tile using the framed tile’s non-gray color; if it does not match, leave it unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of four equal-sized square blocks arranged in a 2x2 or 3x2 layout, separated by rows/columns of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is a compact grid whose dimensions equal the number of block rows by the number of block columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero block, determine whether its cells are mostly filled or mostly empty by comparing the number of colored cells to the number of zero cells in that block. Then build the output as a summary matrix where each position contains the original block color if the block is mostly filled, and 0 if the block is mostly empty.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing blue cells (1) on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of blue cells in the input, then create a centered red shape in the output whose filled cells equal that count: for 1 fill the top-left cell, for 2 fill the top row left-to-right, for 3 fill the top row, for 4 fill the top row plus the center cell, and for 5 or more continue filling outward in the same fixed centered order.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more closed loop shapes drawn with a single nonzero color on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find every enclosed region of black cells that is fully surrounded by the colored линии/loops. Recolor all cells inside each closed shape with green, while keeping the boundary cells unchanged and turning all other background cells outside the closed shapes green as well.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a small colored object near the center-left and one or more single-cell colored “antennae” cells extending away from it in a straight line.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the antenna cell that is farthest from the main object and use it as the starting point. From that antenna, continue drawing the same color one cell at a time in the same direction until reaching the opposite corner or the edge of the grid, leaving existing colored cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is an n×n grid with a single colored pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 2n×2n.
Describing how to transform the grid: Create four copies of the input and place them in a 2×2 arrangement, but rotate each copy around the center of the 2n×2n canvas: top-left is the original grid, top-right is rotated 90° clockwise, bottom-right is rotated 180°, and bottom-left is rotated 270° clockwise.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 3x3 arrangement of 3x3 color blocks, where one block acts as the reference block and the others are copies that may be partially missing; the missing cells indicate the local pattern to be used.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 9x9, formed by replacing each block position with a 3x3 pattern.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the 3x3 occupancy pattern of the large block arrangement, treating each occupied block as a single unit. For each occupied unit, write a 3x3 subgrid whose black cells correspond to the missing cells in the large block, and whose colored cells are filled with 5. In other words, the missing part of the large block determines the missing part of the small block, and this same derived 3x3 mask is tiled into every occupied block position.

Describing the input grid: The grid includes a colored pattern and a gray point that acts as an anchor for a copy operation.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same shape as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the smallest rectangular bounding box containing all nonzero, non-gray cells in the source pattern. Copy that rectangle and paste the copy so that its top-left cell is the gray point’s position, preserving all colors and relative positions inside the rectangle.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large canvas containing a small colored template row-pattern and several translated copies of that pattern placed at regular vertical intervals.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same width as the input and a height equal to the number of repeated copies of the template.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the smallest non-empty row block as the template, then repeat that block downward the required number of times using the same spacing and color, filling the output entirely with the repeated motif.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing a sparse 3x3 pattern that acts as a template for a larger repeated design.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the 3x3 template as a mask and repeat it across the 9x9 grid by translating it to every 3x3 block position; in each translated copy, preserve the colored cells exactly where the template has color and leave the other cells blank.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing several 2x2 monochrome squares placed at various coordinates.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect each 2x2 square and overwrite it with a 2x2 numbered pattern: 1 in the upper-left cell, 2 in the upper-right, 3 in the lower-left, and 4 in the lower-right; all background cells stay black.

Describing the input grid: The input is composed of multiple 3x3 subgrids separated by black space; each subgrid represents one face of a cube-like object, and one of the subgrids is the target position to be completed.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is an 8x8 or 9x9 grid formed from the non-empty subgrid content only, with all-black padding removed.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the non-black 3x3 patterns, identify which one is the source cube face, rotate that face to match the target orientation, and paste it into the missing location. The final output is the compact arrangement of the completed cube faces, with all empty rows and columns removed.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 binary grids, one above and one below a full row of 4s that acts as a separator; the top grid uses 1s and the bottom grid uses 2s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the top 4x4 grid and the bottom 4x4 grid cell-by-cell, treating 1 and 2 as opposite colors that cancel when they disagree. For each position, if both grids have the same value (both 0 or both filled), output 0; if exactly one of them is filled, output 3.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with a few nonzero “seed” cells, each seed having its own color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero seed cell, draw a horizontal stroke to the right starting at that cell and continuing to the last column. Then, at the last column, continue the stroke downward from that row to the bottom of the grid, using the same color. Existing nonzero cells remain unchanged unless they are overwritten by the stroke.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a background color with one connected non-background shape made of several colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the tight bounding box around that non-background shape.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the smallest rectangle that contains every cell whose color is not the background color, and keep the cells inside it exactly as they appear, replacing the background color by 0 in the cropped result.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a cup outline with a teal fill whose height varies, leaving some empty layers above it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Ignore the grey outline and map the teal fill to a 3x3 occupancy diagram by counting the filled interior columns from left to right and the filled interior rows from bottom to top. For each of the three output rows, mark teal in the columns that are reached by the current fill height; all cells above the fill height remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of separate 3x3 colored patterns where the nonzero cells describe a shape and the color acts as a label for which shape to reproduce.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is a 3x3 block-matrix whose cells are themselves 3x3 cells, producing an output that is 3 times larger in each dimension than the arrangement of the input patterns.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each detected 3x3 pattern, convert it to a 3x3 binary stencil and then expand every filled cell into a 3x3 square of the same color; finally, assemble these expanded stencils into the output according to the spatial order implied by the input, so that shape and color are separated into structure versus fill.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 2D field of background cells with one or more rectangular green regions, each containing a single red marker cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each green rectangle, use the red marker as the launch point and the rectangle’s width as the firing width. Fill a horizontal or vertical ray starting from the red marker in the direction indicated by the rectangle’s placement relative to the whole canvas: if the marker is in an upper region, extend the ray downward; if it is in a lower region, extend upward; if it is in a left region, extend rightward; if it is in a right region, extend leftward. The ray is drawn in green until it reaches the boundary of the object region, and any cells already occupied by red remain red.

Describing the input grid: The input is composed of rectangular color blocks on a black background, where each block is a filled rectangle.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every filled rectangle by a tiled motif of size 2 colors around a red center: draw blue only on the four corner cells of the rectangle, yellow on all edge cells excluding corners, and red on all non-edge interior cells. The original block color is ignored, and empty black cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a large grid with several regions of 8s, and one small “seed” pattern made of non-8 colors embedded in the lower middle area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every 0 cell by alternating colors from the seed pattern, extending the seed’s row pattern left-to-right across each row and repeating it downward in a checker-like alternating way; keep all original 8s unchanged and preserve any non-8 seed cells as anchors.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more horizontal rows of colored cells near the bottom, with empty space above them. The lowest colored row acts as an anchor, and the row above it may contain a shorter colored segment.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the bottom colored row unchanged. Then, for every colored cell in the row immediately above it, place the same color in the two cells diagonally above-left and above-right of that cell, but only if those cells are currently empty. This creates a symmetric expansion starting from two corners of the lower pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 7x7 grid with four 2x2 corner patterns, and the middle row/column act as spacers that separate the corners.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output by taking the outer boundary of the four corner patterns as if they were the four visible corners of a cube: the top-left corner fills the upper-left of the 3x3, the top-right corner fills the upper-right, the bottom-left corner fills the lower-left, and the bottom-right corner fills the lower-right, while the center cell is colored only if any corner pattern contributes to the center position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several separate 5-colored square frames of different sizes, each with a hollow interior.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each closed square frame and measure its side length. Fill the hollow interior of each square with a new color determined by the square’s size: one size maps to teal, another size maps to orange, and the largest size maps to fuchsia. Keep the original 5-colored border unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several rectangular sections of the same width, separated by a full horizontal line of a single color that runs all the way across the grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same height and width as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the full-width separator line. Recolor every cell in that separator line to color 2, leaving all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains several colors, but one color appears as a repeated “marked” color that is the target of the transformation.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the color that occurs most often in the grid; replace every cell of that most frequent color with grey (5), leaving all other colors unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a large rectangular tiling made of repeated color blocks, with one color in the grid forming a solid rectangle that acts as a separator or marker line.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 7x7 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the unique solid rectangle color in the input and use it as the border color of the output. Then extract the 5x5 region of the repeating pattern that lies immediately adjacent to the rectangle marker and center it inside the border, replacing all non-border cells with the colors from that selected pattern region.

Describing the input grid: Each row has a “label color” in column 0, and the rest of the row contains one or more copied templates made of nonzero cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same shape as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat every nonzero cell in columns 1 onward as a placeholder to be recolored by the row label: if the row’s first cell is 1, 2, 3, or 4, recolor all nonzero cells in that row to that same value; do not alter the 0 cells or the first column.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a square grid with several isolated nonzero points, all having the same color, and the rest are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every nonzero point by a 3x3 filled square of color 1 centered on that point. If these squares overlap, keep them as color 1; all other cells remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a small square grid containing a few nonzero colored “seed” cells, with the rest black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero seed cell, copy its color straight downward within its own column to every cell below it until the bottom of the grid; if multiple seeds affect the same cell, the lower seed’s color takes precedence over any color coming from above.

Describing the input grid: The input contains repeated blocks of a uniform color with isolated differently colored cells acting as seeds.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each seed cell, determine which of the four corners of its block is closest in Manhattan distance, then fill the cells on the straight diagonal path from the seed to that corner with the seed’s color; if multiple seeds affect the same cell, the later or closer seed is not used and the first painted color remains.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of isolated colored points on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored point, draw a repeated checker-like expansion pattern using that color: fill cells where the number of steps from the seed in the vertical direction is at most the seed’s row index, and the horizontal reach alternates by parity of the distance from the seed. The expansion continues until the grid boundary, and overlapping expansions from different seeds erase each other to black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains a 2×2 green block plus several isolated colored points. These points act as endpoints that define row and column lines through the green block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the rows and columns that intersect the 2×2 green block. For each isolated colored point, draw a straight line of that point’s color along its row or column until it reaches the nearest row or column of the green block, but only in the direction that creates an intersection with the green block. If multiple points share the same row or column, their drawn segments may overlap. Preserve the green block.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a periodic pattern in the bottom three rows, where each 5-row column slice repeats after a constant horizontal shift, while the top rows stay all zero.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect the repeating period from the non-zero portion of the bottom three rows, then extend the pattern by copying the observed period rightward across the entire row region, leaving the zero rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x6 grid split into a left 3x3 half and a right 3x3 half, with the left half using color 4 and the right half using color 3.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the left 3x3 half and the right 3x3 half cell-by-cell; if either half has a nonzero cell at a position, write 6 in the output at that position, otherwise write 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a set of colored reference cells along the top row, and several rectangular blocks of a different color placed below them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Partition the grid into regions determined by the intersections of the block locations with the top-row reference columns. Every solid block is recolored according to the top-row color in the same column interval, while cells outside the blocks remain unchanged. The block color is thus replaced by the corresponding top marker color for that block’s column position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one short diagonal/angled arrangement of colored cells that encodes a repeating sequence of colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the ordered list of nonzero colors from the seed arrangement, then replicate that list periodically along the full horizontal line passing through the seed’s rightmost/central point and vertically along the full column passing through the seed’s bottommost/central point. All positions not on those propagated lines remain 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x9 grid divided by a central column of 5s into two 10x4 panels.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 10x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the right panel horizontally and use it as a mask to complete the left panel. The output is formed by taking the left panel and, at every cell where the mirrored right panel has a nonzero value, replacing the left cell with that mirrored value; cells where the mirrored right panel is zero remain unchanged. The fold line itself is discarded.

Describing the input grid: The input shows two same-sized grids stacked or placed side-by-side with a separator between them, where one grid is mostly a uniform color and the other grid contains a sparse pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the size of a single grid after the copy is performed.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the two grids cell by cell and transfer the non-background cells from one grid to the other at the same relative positions. The result is the target grid with the transferred cells inserted, while all other cells remain as the target grid’s background color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a set of 5-colored perimeters and partial perimeters on a black background, where some interior spaces are not fully enclosed but are still identifiable as the area "inside" the edge structure.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each connected component of 5-cells as a boundary system, then fill the black cells that are on the interior side of that boundary system with 4. Interior side means the black cells that are not reachable from the outside of the grid without crossing a 5, even if the boundary has openings elsewhere. Leave all boundary 5s unchanged and do not fill exterior black cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid made of background color 5 and several disconnected regions of 0 cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find every connected component of 0-cells in the input using 4-directional adjacency. Recolor each component according to its size: components of size 1 become color 1, size 2 become color 2, size 3 become color 3. Leave all nonzero cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a top pattern, a separator row of 5s, and a bottom pattern of the same dimensions; the task is to merge the two patterns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps only one panel, with the same dimensions as either the top or bottom section.
Describing how to transform the grid: Remove the separator row, then overlay the bottom panel onto the top panel cell-by-cell; wherever either panel has a nonzero value, copy that color into the output. If both panels have nonzero values in the same position, keep the color from the top panel unless the top is black, in which case use the bottom color.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains a colored perimeter-like shape and a separate colored interior region; the perimeter color determines where a copy of the interior color should be projected.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored structure, detect the color that appears inside its enclosed area. Starting from the outer corner nearest the empty space, “shoot” that inner color diagonally away from the structure until it reaches the first empty cells outside the frame, placing only the projected cells and not modifying the original colors.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid containing one non-black color and black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Rotate the entire grid 90 degrees counterclockwise.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a grid with a repeated diagonal/step-like arrangement of one color and a few isolated cells of another color that act as anchors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fill the grid by tiling the visible 4-row motif downward. Each time the pattern reaches a row containing the anchor color, shift the next copy of the motif left by one cell relative to the previous copy. The result is a staggered repetition of the original sparse pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid whose cells are all either background or the same nonzero color, and it can be partitioned into 2×2 blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has half the height and half the width of the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Divide the input into non-overlapping 2×2 blocks. For each block, if at least one cell in the block is nonzero, write that same nonzero color into the corresponding single cell of the output; otherwise write 0. This is a 2×2 downsampling by presence of color.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a tall grid whose top half is all background, and whose bottom half contains a vertically symmetric pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the bottom half of the input grid into the top half by flipping it vertically, so the entire grid becomes symmetric across the horizontal midline.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a sparse grid of nonzero points on a 0 background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is exactly twice the height and twice the width of the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build a 2×2 tiled copy of the input grid. In each tile, replace every row that contains a nonzero point in the original grid with color 8 across the full width of that tile, and replace every column that contains a nonzero point in the original grid with color 8 across the full height of that tile; the nonzero point locations themselves remain their original colors.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid where one color acts as background and the other color marks a set of positions to be selected.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: First recolor all non-background cells to gray. Then keep only the gray cells that belong to the cells where the row index equals the column index or where the row index plus the column index equals the grid size minus one; set all other cells to background.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid containing several 2×1 blocks arranged in rows, where each row of blocks has one dominant color repeated across most blocks and one outlier color appearing in exactly one block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3×1 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Partition the grid into three block-rows, and in each block-row identify the color that appears least often among the 2×1 blocks after ignoring background zeros; output that least frequent nonzero color as one cell, from the top block-row to the bottom block-row.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two 3x3 binary masks stacked vertically, separated into a top mask using 9 as the foreground color and a bottom mask using 1 as the foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the two 3x3 masks by treating 9 and 1 as the same occupied cell. Then take the complement of the overlay: cells occupied in either mask become 0, and cells occupied in neither mask become 2.

Describing the input grid: The input contains multiple separated 2x2 objects, some monochrome and some bicolored.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every 2x2 object, count the number of unique colors appearing in that object. Starting from the row just below the object, copy a 2x2 green placeholder downward for a number of rows equal to that count, aligned to the same 2x2 position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains three regions separated by a solid horizontal divider: a top pattern, a middle divider row, and a bottom pattern. The top and bottom patterns are related by central symmetry around the center of the whole grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is a square matching one quadrant-sized pattern region.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top and bottom patterns cell by cell under 180-degree rotation. Mark in the output the cells that belong to the symmetric overlap of the two patterns: a cell is colored if the corresponding cell in the top half and its centrally symmetric partner in the bottom half are both nonzero; otherwise set it to 0.

Describing the input grid: A black background contains connected colored objects whose occupied rows form slanted hollow shapes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each connected colored object, leave its lowest occupied row unchanged, but horizontally squeeze all rows above it into a bounding box whose left edge is one column farther right and whose right edge is unchanged, preserving row positions and colors.

Describing the input grid: The input contains exactly two non-black single cells on an otherwise black grid; their border positions indicate whether they are column clues or row clues.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: If the clues are on the top/bottom borders, turn them into full vertical columns; if the clues are on the left/right borders, turn them into full horizontal rows. Use the distance between the two clue positions along that chosen axis as the spacing, alternate the two clue colors, and continue the repeating stripes only in the positive direction from the earlier clue position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several non-black colors on a black background, with one non-black color appearing much less often overall than the other non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding box around all cells of the least frequent non-black color.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of cells of each non-black color, choose the color with the smallest total count, crop the smallest rectangle containing all cells of that color, and preserve the black cells inside that crop.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid where each column is a solid vertical stripe, and the same 3-color row is repeated three times.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every cell in the same position, but replace colors using fixed swap pairs: 1↔5, 2↔6, 3↔4, and 8↔9.

Describing the input grid: The input is a set of repeated vertical column patterns, with some entries in some columns erased to black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Group columns that are compatible, meaning that every row where both columns are non-black has the same value. For each column group, merge the non-black values to reconstruct the full column template. Replace every column by its completed template, filling the black cells.

Describing the input grid: A black grid contains a single grey object that can be interpreted as larger “rooms” connected by narrow “corridors.”
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid dimensions are unchanged.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep black background cells unchanged. Identify the maximal compact regions of the grey object that have thickness 2 in both directions, i.e. the room-like 2x2 square chunks, and color those cells teal 8. Color every grey cell that lies in a one-cell-wide corridor, bridge, or protruding line red 2.

Describing the input grid: The input contains colored point markers indicating selected rows and columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use red 2 markers as column selectors and color those full columns red. Use all non-red markers, blue 1 and green 3, as row selectors and color those full rows with the marker’s color. Row selectors have priority over column selectors at intersections.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one complete, axis-aligned, single-color rectangular frame embedded in a larger noisy grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the height and width of that rectangle minus its border: frame height - 2 by frame width - 2.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the monochrome rectangular outline, ignore the outline itself, and output exactly the cells strictly inside the rectangle, preserving their colors and positions.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing one 2x2 non-black marker made of red cells and one other color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the non-red color C. Replace the whole 2x2 marker area with color C, then copy that solid 2x2 square repeatedly in each diagonal direction indicated by a red cell’s corner position: top-left means northwest, top-right means northeast, bottom-left means southwest, and bottom-right means southeast. Continue copying until the copies leave the grid, clipping at the edges. All copied cells are color C; all other cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large black grid containing several non-black colors, where one color forms the target object and the other colors are scattered noise.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding box around all cells of the least frequent non-black color.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the total number of cells of each non-black color, select the color with the smallest count, discard all other colors, and crop to the smallest rectangle containing every cell of the selected color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 black grid containing several pairs of same-colored cells; each pair lies on a 45-degree diagonal.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 10x10 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color, find its two cells and draw the straight diagonal line segment connecting them, filling every black cell between them with that same color.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more colored V-shaped or wedge-shaped outlines on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if there are two cells of the same non-black color in that row, fill every black cell horizontally between those two colored cells with that same color. Leave all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with colored cells placed only on the far left and far right edges, with black cells elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if the leftmost cell and rightmost cell are the same non-black color, fill the entire row with that color. Leave all other rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains black background cells, teal cells, and exactly two red 2x2 blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 1x1.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat teal cells as path cells using 4-directional adjacency. If any single connected teal component touches both red 2x2 blocks orthogonally, output [[8]]. Otherwise output [[0]].

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains black background cells and some teal cells acting as markers.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all teal cells unchanged. For every pair of teal cells that lie in the same row or the same column, draw a straight green line through the black cells strictly between them. Do not draw anything between diagonal teal cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if all 3 cells in that row have the same color, make the entire corresponding output row grey. Otherwise, make the entire corresponding output row black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing some non-black cells of a single color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Move every non-black cell down by exactly one row, keeping its column and color unchanged; cells moved from row 3 would fall off the grid, and all other cells become black.

Describing the input grid: The input is an empty black square, and only its side length matters.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same square size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recursively draw nested green frames. For a current square region, color its top edge, right edge, bottom edge, and the left edge except for the cell directly below the top-left corner. Also color the connector cell two rows below and one column right of the region’s top-left corner when it exists. Then repeat the same operation inside the sub-square obtained by moving 2 cells inward from the top-left and 2 cells inward from the bottom-right, until no sub-square remains.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one large solid rectangle and some scattered single cells whose color is different from both the rectangle and the background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Look at every row and column. If a row contains both rectangle cells and one or more scattered marker cells, fill the background cells between the rectangle and each such marker with the marker color. If a column contains both rectangle cells and one or more scattered marker cells, fill the background cells between the rectangle and each such marker with the marker color. Rows or columns that do not contain part of the rectangle are not extended.

Describing the input grid: The input is split by the yellow row into two equal 6x5 binary masks, where red cells are filled and black cells are empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as one mask, 6x5.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each position, count how many of the two masks have a red cell there. If the count is odd, place a green cell in the output. If the count is even, place a black cell.

Describing the input grid: The input is a regular lattice of blank compartments, with some compartments filled by colored blocks; one non-separator color acts as a seed and another as the fill color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Determine which one-step neighborhood positions around a seed compartment are already occupied by the fill color anywhere in the grid. Then apply this learned local rule to every seed compartment: every black compartment at one of those relative neighborhood positions becomes the fill color, while all existing colored cells and separator lines stay unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid where the first row contains one or more colored markers and all lower rows are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the first row unchanged. Create a second-row pattern by shifting every colored cell from the first row one step left and one step right, preserving its color and ignoring shifts outside the grid. Then repeat these two rows alternately down the entire grid.

Describing the input grid: A square grid contains a single colored column on the far left, and the number of rows equals the number of columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input, then for each row r except the last row, place a red cell in column n-1-r; finally, fill every cell in the last row to the right of the original left column with yellow.

Describing the input grid: The input contains four same-colored marker cells forming the corners of an axis-aligned rectangle, with another colored pattern inside that rectangle.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output height is the number of rows strictly between the top and bottom marker rows, and the output width is the number of columns strictly between the left and right marker columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the area strictly inside the four markers; every non-black cell in that cropped area becomes the marker color, and every black cell remains black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more blue container-like outlines, each with a single non-blue colored cell inside it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored cell inside a blue outline, use that cell’s color to fill the empty interior of its blue container: fill all black cells between the left and right blue walls on rows above the bottom wall, fill the opening in the top blue row, and draw a horizontal line of the same color one row above the container spanning the full container width. Keep all blue cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains grey connected components with black background cells, including some black regions trapped inside grey components.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every enclosed black connected component, count its cells. If the number of cells is a perfect square, recolor that entire enclosed component red. Otherwise leave it black. All grey cells remain grey.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grid where the meaningful information is stored in the bottom-right cell of each 2x2 region.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 20x20.
Describing how to transform the grid: Sample only the even-numbered rows and even-numbered columns of the input to form a 5x5 miniature grid. Then upscale that 5x5 grid by replacing each sampled cell with a solid 4x4 block of the same color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with multiple colored 3x3 patterns and a single grey cell acting as a pointer.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the grey cell. Take the 3x3 region whose top-middle cell is immediately below the grey cell: the rows are the three rows below the grey cell, and the columns are one left of, equal to, and one right of the grey cell’s column. Output that 3x3 region.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a black background, a 2x2 green block, and one or more red patterns placed in quadrant positions around that green block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the 2x2 green block as the center mirror. For every red cell, add its horizontal mirror across the green block, its vertical mirror across the green block, and its mirror across both axes. Keep the green block unchanged and leave all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a tall grid with one or more complete colored horizontal rows at the top, followed by black rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the input unchanged, then reflect every non-black cell across the horizontal middle of the whole grid, placing a mirrored copy at the corresponding row from the bottom.

Describing the input grid: The input is a colored grid where the relevant colors first appear in a consistent sequence when read from the top-left.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output contains each distinct input color exactly once; make it a row if the input has more columns than rows, otherwise make it a column.
Describing how to transform the grid: Scan the input in normal reading order, row by row from left to right. Record each new color the first time it appears, ignoring later repeats. Place those recorded colors in the output in that order.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a small symmetric 3x3 X-shaped object and another larger colored shape located in one quadrant around it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the center of the X-shaped object as an origin. For each cell of the other colored shape, compute its row and column offset from that center. Place that color at all four positions formed by independently changing the signs of the row offset and column offset. Leave the X-shaped object in place.

Describing the input grid: The input contains scattered black/grey background cells and red cells that are clues lying on the middle row and middle column of hidden odd-sized squares.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Partition the red clues into groups that can be covered by a single odd square, possibly clipped by the edge of the grid, whose two medians contain all red cells in that group. For each such square, draw its horizontal and vertical median lines; any median cell that is not already red becomes teal 8, and all non-median background/noise cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains solid colored blocks made from repeated same-color cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input, then find every 3x3 subgrid that is entirely filled with one nonblack color. For each such 3x3 subgrid, recolor its center cell to teal 8. All other cells keep their original values.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains colored cells and black cells, including black “gap” cells that are surrounded on the left, right, and above by cells of the same non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input grid unchanged, then find every black cell whose immediate left, immediate right, and immediate upper neighbors are all the same non-black color. For each such black gap cell, place a yellow cell in the bottom row in the same column.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 black grid containing one 2x2 blue block and one 2x2 red block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 10x10 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep both 2x2 blocks unchanged. From the upper-left corner of the blue block, draw a blue diagonal ray upward-left until it reaches the grid edge. From the lower-right corner of the red block, draw a red diagonal ray downward-right until it reaches the grid edge.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with exactly three non-black cells of one color, forming a diagonal arithmetic progression.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the central colored cell and call its row and column `(cr, cc)`. Let `d` be the absolute row difference between adjacent input dots. For every cell `(r, c)`, compute `m = max(abs(r-cr), abs(c-cc))`. Color the cell with the input color if `m` is `0` or a positive multiple of `d`; otherwise make it black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a framed window made by the intersections of two complete horizontal colored lines and two complete vertical colored lines, with scattered colored markers; the useful markers are those inside the window whose color is also the color of one window border.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is exactly the framed window, including its colored border lines.
Describing how to transform the grid: Within the window, treat every marker that has the same color as a border as being attracted to that same-colored border. Repeatedly copy that marker one cell at a time toward the matching border, leaving a colored trail behind it, until the trail reaches the border. Remove/ignore any markers outside the window, and leave the other interior cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains separated groups of yellow cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each yellow group, identify the rows and columns occupied by that group. Any black cell whose row and column are both occupied by that same group is changed to orange, while the original yellow cells remain yellow and all other black cells stay black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single colored point on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the original dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every cell in the grid, compare its coordinates to the coordinates of the colored input point. Color the cell with the same color if it has either the same row-minus-column value or the same row-plus-column value as the input point; otherwise leave it black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains multiple colored connected components, with black as background and grey as a special replacement color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Ignore the grey object and examine the normalized shape of every other colored connected component. Find the pair of components with identical normalized cell patterns in the same orientation. Recolor the later-occurring member of that duplicate pair, scanning from top to bottom and left to right, to grey. Leave everything else unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is an 11x11 grid divided by teal separator lines into nine 3x3 compartments; the meaningful cells inside the compartments are fuchsia dots on black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3, with one cell corresponding to each 3x3 compartment of the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of fuchsia cells in each 3x3 compartment. If a compartment contains exactly two fuchsia cells, put a blue cell in the corresponding output position; otherwise put black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains nonzero cells forming a full horizontal stripe and a full vertical stripe, with the two stripes meeting at one shared position.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the row of the horizontal stripe and the column of the vertical stripe. For every cell whose row is within 1 of that stripe row and whose column is within 1 of that stripe column, change it to yellow, except for the exact intersection cell, which keeps its original color. All other cells remain as in the input.

Describing the input grid: The input is a noisy 20x20 grid where hidden solid black 3x3 square regions appear among cells of one foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify maximal empty square regions of size 3x3: a valid region is a 3x3 square of black cells that is not simply a down-right shifted overlap of another all-black 3x3 square starting one row above and one column to the left. Recolor each valid 3x3 black region blue, preserving all other black and colored cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing non-black colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of distinct colors in the input. If there is 1 distinct color, draw a grey horizontal line across the top row. If there are 2 distinct colors, draw a grey main diagonal from top-left to bottom-right. If there are 3 distinct colors, draw a grey anti-diagonal from top-right to bottom-left. All other cells become black.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains examples of symmetric three-cell color triples, where two matching outer colors surround a different center color in a row, column, or diagonal.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Learn every complete A-B-A triple orientation that appears in the input. For each learned triple, complete all matching incomplete triples in the same orientation: if the center B is present alone, add the two outer A cells; if the two outer A cells are present with the center missing, add B; if one side of an A-B-A triple is missing, add the missing A. Multiple learned triples may combine at the same center, producing plus-shapes or X-shapes.

Describing the input grid: A symmetric colored table has been partially covered by black rectangular holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Restore the holes by symmetry across the main diagonal: any black cell whose transposed partner is non-black takes that transposed color. After doing this, any remaining black cell on the main diagonal takes the diagonal color, and any remaining off-diagonal black cell takes the background/off-diagonal color immediately preceding the diagonal color in the cyclic palette.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one non-black rectangular block made from two colors, surrounded by black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding rectangle around the non-black block.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop out the non-black rectangle, then reflect that cropped rectangle horizontally, so the left and right columns are swapped while the row order and colors stay the same.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains one complete grey rectangular frame with a colored prototype shape inside it, plus several separate blue shapes outside the frame.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the non-grey, non-black colored object inside the complete grey rectangular frame. Then find every blue connected component outside the frame whose cropped cell pattern is exactly the same shape in the same orientation. Recolor those matching blue components to the prototype’s color, leaving all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a set of rectangular color panels separated by complete black rows and complete black columns; each panel contains one non-black color mixed with black holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has one row for each horizontal band of panels and one column for each vertical band of panels.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input at every all-black row and all-black column. For each resulting rectangular panel, find its non-black color and place that color in the corresponding position of the smaller output grid.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid containing some blue cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of blue cells. Replace them with the same number of red cells placed in a fixed order: top-left, top-middle, top-right, then center.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing a network of one non-black color made from horizontal and vertical line segments.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all original non-black line cells unchanged. Treat those line cells as walls, then flood-fill through black cells from the outside border using 4-directional movement. Change every black cell reachable from the border to green 3, and change every remaining black cell, which is enclosed by the walls, to red 2.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a same-colored 2x2 square and extra colored pixels positioned on diagonals aligned with corners of that square.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same height and width as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each extra colored pixel, use its position relative to the 2x2 square to define a one-step diagonal translation: up-left, up-right, down-left, or down-right. Repeatedly copy that pixel by this translation vector, preserving the color, until the next copy would fall outside the grid. All original colored cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x6.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the output into four 3x3 quadrants. Put the original input in the upper-left quadrant, the input rotated 90 degrees clockwise in the upper-right quadrant, the input rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise in the lower-left quadrant, and the input rotated 180 degrees in the lower-right quadrant.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a grey shape made from 3x3 solid grey blocks inside a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the tight 9x9 bounding box around the grey shape, divide it into a 3x3 grid of 3x3 blocks, and record which blocks are grey to form a 3x3 pattern. Then replace every grey cell of that 3x3 pattern with a copy of the whole 3x3 pattern, and replace every black cell with a 3x3 black block.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one multicolored non-grey object and one grey marker cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the entire multicolored object without changing its orientation. Place the copy so that the center of the object’s bounding box lands on the grey marker cell. The grey marker is overwritten by the copied object’s corresponding color, while the original object remains unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with one non-black color forming a contiguous 3-row horizontal motif, with empty black rows above and/or below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the 3 non-empty rows as a repeating vertical tile. Fill every row of the output by repeating those 3 rows upward and downward, preserving the original phase: output row `r` is the extracted motif row corresponding to `(r - top_of_motif) mod 3`.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing one monochrome 3x3 pattern placed somewhere on the black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the non-black 3x3 pattern. Make a 3x3 array of 3x3 blocks: wherever the cropped pattern has a colored cell, place a full copy of the cropped pattern in the corresponding 3x3 block; wherever it has a black cell, leave that 3x3 block black.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more separate 2x2 grey squares on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every grey 2x2 square unchanged. For each grey square, place four colored cells diagonally just outside its corners: blue at the upper-left outside corner, red at the upper-right outside corner, green at the lower-left outside corner, and yellow at the lower-right outside corner.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a red canvas containing several black cutout shapes; outside the canvas are colored 3x3 masks where red marks the cutout shape and the other color marks what should be painted.
Describing the size of the output grid: Crop the output to exactly the rectangular red canvas, excluding all outside clue masks.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every 3x3 black cutout area in the red canvas, choose the outside mask whose red-cell pattern can be rotated to coincide exactly with the black cells of that cutout. Then paint every other cell of that same 3x3 area with the mask’s non-red color, leaving all remaining cells red.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two aligned 4x4 binary masks separated by a yellow row: the upper mask uses blue for filled cells, and the lower mask uses red for filled cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 4 rows by 4 columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat blue and red cells as filled and black cells as empty. Add the two aligned masks using parity: a cell becomes green if the total number of filled cells in that position across the two masks is odd, and black if the total is even.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing one or more isolated colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored cell, draw an L-shape in that same color: extend horizontally from the cell to the right edge, then extend vertically downward along the rightmost column to the bottom edge. If L-shapes overlap, cells from lower starting points overwrite cells from higher starting points.

Describing the input grid: A set of colored non-blue cells placed on a blue empty field.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has height equal to the vertical span of the non-blue cells and width equal to the horizontal span of the non-blue cells.
Describing how to transform the grid: Translate all non-blue cells together so that the topmost occupied row and leftmost occupied column become the top and left edges of the output, keep their colors and relative positions, and fill all unoccupied positions with black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a grey container with a rectangular teal liquid region at its bottom and black space above it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Measure the vertical depth of the liquid surface from the container opening: subtract the row index of the top grey wall from the row index of the topmost teal cell. Draw that many teal cells in unary on the 3x3 grid, using the order top-left, top-middle, top-right, middle-right, middle-middle, middle-left, bottom-left, bottom-middle, bottom-right; all unused cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two same-sized square panels placed side by side: one panel has colored non-teal cells, and the other panel contains a teal 8-pattern used as a stencil.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output height is the colored panel height multiplied by the stencil height, and the output width is the colored panel width multiplied by the stencil width.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero non-teal cell in the colored panel, replace that cell with a copy of the teal stencil recolored to that cell’s color; zero cells become all-black blocks.

Describing the input grid: The input grid shows green rectangular blocks on a black background, with each block having exactly one red cell embedded on its boundary.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each red cell as the start of a “laser beam” pointing directly away from the center of its containing rectangle, perpendicular to the rectangle side it lies on. Draw that beam to the outer border of the grid. Then thicken the beam equally on both sides by a radius equal to the distance from the red cell to the opposite side of its rectangle. Color the center beam red and the thickened surrounding cells green, without altering the original block.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with grey cells arranged into solid blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every grey cell, count its grey orthogonal neighbors up, down, left, and right. If it has 2 grey orthogonal neighbors, make it blue; if it has 3, make it yellow; if it has 4, make it red. Leave black cells black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains teal barrier cells, black open cells, and a few pre-colored cells using exactly two non-teal colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat every non-teal cell as open space and find the 4-connected open component containing the pre-colored cells. The colored cells define a checkerboard coloring by row-plus-column parity: one clue color belongs to one parity and the other clue color belongs to the opposite parity. Fill every black cell in that same component according to this parity rule, while leaving all other open components black and keeping teal cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a two-color object resting near the bottom; its uppermost non-black row is a horizontal run or single cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all existing cells. Find the left and right ends of the uppermost non-black horizontal run, then draw 45-degree diagonal rays upward-left from the left end and upward-right from the right end. Color the added cells with the contrasting color that appears in the middle of the bottom row of the object.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 5x7 grid with colored cells only in the outer two columns on each side, using a single non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build each output cell from fixed input positions: output corners copy the four extreme input corners, output edge-centers are the OR/union of the two input cells on the matching separated edge, and the center is the OR/union of the four inner corner cells adjacent to the central gap. More explicitly, use input positions (0,0) for output (0,0), (0,1) or (0,5) for (0,1), (0,6) for (0,2), (1,0) or (3,0) for (1,0), (1,1) or (1,5) or (3,1) or (3,5) for (1,1), (1,6) or (3,6) for (1,2), (4,0) for (2,0), (4,1) or (4,5) for (2,1), and (4,6) for (2,2).

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with grey closed rectangular/square outlines that enclose black interior cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each closed grey outline, count the number of black cells completely enclosed by it. If it encloses 1 cell, color that cell 6; if it encloses 4 cells, color them 7; if it encloses 9 cells, color them 8. All grey cells and outside black cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid containing one main nonzero color, black background cells, and some complete black separator lines.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find every row and every column that is entirely black in the input. Recolor all cells in those full-black rows and columns to red 2. Leave every other cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A rectangular grid containing blue 1, teal 8, and orange 7 cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input grid, but replace every orange 7 cell with grey 5; leave all blue 1 and teal 8 cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains many repeated colors plus one rare color that appears as a connected rectangular outline.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the bounding rectangle of that rare-color outline, here 7x7.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the connected component made from the rare outline color, take its minimum and maximum row and column positions, and copy that entire rectangular subgrid from the input as the output.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 12x12 grid with colored cells along the left edge and grey shapes elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each colored cell in the leftmost column as projecting its color horizontally across its row. Wherever that horizontal projection passes through a grey cell, recolor that grey cell with the projecting color. Leave black cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid that can be divided into nine non-overlapping 3x3 blocks, with some blocks containing a grey marker.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid, 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 block, if that block contains at least one grey cell, fill the entire corresponding 3x3 block in the output with blue. If a 3x3 block contains no grey cell, leave it black.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid containing colored cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Repeatedly scan downward through each column: whenever a black cell has a colored cell directly above it, change that black cell to the same color. Continue this propagation until no more black cells can inherit a color from above.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid made mostly from two large background colors, with a few rare marker colors embedded inside those backgrounds.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every rare marker cell, draw both full diagonal lines through it, one NW-SE and one NE-SW. Any cell lying on one of those diagonals is recolored according to the background color it originally had: each background color is replaced by the rare marker color that appears inside that background color. Cells not on any drawn diagonal stay unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input has colored cells only in some corners, acting as sources, with all other cells black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Assign every cell to the active corner source with the smallest Euclidean distance; if two active sources are equally close, output black there. For a cell assigned to a corner, compute the smallest axis-aligned square with that corner as one corner and the cell on its outer edge. If that square has odd side length, fill the cell with the corner’s color; if the side length is even, leave it black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing one 2x2 green square and several isolated colored pixels of one or two other colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the green square and all original colored pixels. For every non-green colored pixel that lies in the same row or same column as any cell of the green square, draw a straight horizontal or vertical line of that pixel’s color through the black cells between that pixel and the green square. Pixels not row- or column-aligned with the green square do not create lines.

Describing the input grid: A fixed-height grid contains a left-aligned colored pattern, with the right side mostly empty black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the smallest horizontal period width p such that the already-drawn non-black cells are consistent with repeating the first p columns. Then fill the whole grid by copying columns 0 through p-1 repeatedly from left to right until the full width is filled.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3-row by 6-column grid containing a yellow 3x3 pattern on the left and a green 3x3 pattern on the right.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input into two 3x3 halves, overlay the right half onto the left half, and put fuchsia 6 in every cell where either half has a non-black cell; otherwise put black 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with the same number of top-row colored markers as grey connected objects below.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Sort the colored markers from left to right by column, and sort the grey connected objects from left to right by their horizontal center. Recolor the first grey object with the first marker color, the second grey object with the second marker color, and so on, leaving all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a small sample of two infinite periodic stripes: a horizontal stripe and a vertical stripe, intersecting at one colored cell.
