Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the visible nonblack cells in the multi-colored horizontal row as one period of a horizontal color cycle, and treat the visible nonblack cells in the multi-colored vertical column as one period of a vertical color cycle. Extend each cycle across the full grid boundary in its own direction, using modular repetition so the given colored cells remain unchanged. All cells not on either completed stripe remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input is divided by a grey vertical line into four columns on the left and four columns on the right.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has 10 rows and 4 columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, merge each pair of cells equidistant from the grey line: input columns 0 and 8 become output column 0, columns 1 and 7 become output column 1, columns 2 and 6 become output column 2, and columns 3 and 5 become output column 3. If both paired cells are black, output black; otherwise output the non-black color.

Describing the input grid: The grid is divided into two large rectangular panels with different background colors; one panel contains complete colored objects with a main body color and some embedded marker-color cells, while the other panel contains only background plus sparse marker-color cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the sparse-marker panel.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the complete-object panel as a library of templates. For each object, record the relative positions and colors of its embedded marker cells, ignoring the main body color and the panel background. If that exact marker pattern appears translated somewhere in the sparse-marker panel, copy the entire object into the output at that translated position, replacing the sparse markers with the full object. Fill all other cells with the sparse panel’s background color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing grey line drawings made from color 5.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all grey cells unchanged. Find each connected black region that is trapped inside grey walls, allowing the left or right edge of the grid to act as a wall. A trapped region may not connect to the top or bottom edge or to the large outside background. Recolor every cell in such trapped regions yellow, color 4.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grey grid with several black “holes” or islands of black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all grey cells as 5. For every 4-connected black island, compute its outside perimeter using orthogonal edges. A perimeter of 4 becomes color 3, a perimeter of 6 becomes color 2, and a perimeter of 8 becomes color 1. Recolor the whole island with that color.

Describing the input grid: The input is an 11x11 grid split by a horizontal grey divider row into a 5x11 upper panel and a 5x11 lower panel.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 5x11.
Describing how to transform the grid: Remove the grey divider and overlay the lower 5x11 panel directly onto the upper 5x11 panel without shifting horizontally; black cells are transparent, and if both panels have a colored cell in the same position, keep the upper panel’s color.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a large angular outline or wall in one color, plus a smaller colored object separated from that wall by black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the whole input unchanged. Identify the smaller object’s color. From each cell of that smaller object, look along the four diagonal directions. If the diagonal passes through only black cells and then hits a corner cell of the large wall, continue that same diagonal beyond the wall corner, coloring every black cell with the smaller object’s color until the grid edge is reached.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid containing black cells and cells of one non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 3x3 size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Rotate the entire input grid 90 degrees counterclockwise, preserving all cell colors.

Describing the input grid: The input has teal 8 seeds along one edge and red 2 markers along a different edge, with the teal seeds defining starting points for parallel paths.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the exact same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: From every teal 8 seed, draw a straight ray into the grid perpendicular to the edge it starts on. Whenever the ray enters a row or column that contains a red 2 marker, shift the ray one cell sideways in the direction away from the red-marker edge, then continue. Draw all visited ray cells as teal 8, preserve the red 2 cells, and leave all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: A 9x9 black grid containing one yellow shape.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is twice the height and twice the width of the smallest rectangle that contains all yellow cells.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the input to the tight bounding box around the yellow shape, then upscale that cropped pattern by a factor of 2 in both directions, replacing each original cell with a 2x2 block of the same color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x4 grid where the top five rows are empty and the bottom five rows contain the colored pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 10x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the bottom five rows into the top five rows in reverse vertical order, while leaving the original bottom five rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input grid has black background cells and some marked cells of a single non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is twice as tall and twice as wide as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Determine which input columns contain at least one marked cell. Create a doubled grid by repeating the input grid twice across and twice down. In this doubled grid, for every cell whose column position modulo the original width is one of the marked columns, replace black cells with teal 8; leave original repeated colored cells unchanged and leave all other black cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a two-color grid where one color is always grey 5 and the other color varies by case.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Swap grey 5 with the other input color throughout the grid, then erase all remaining grey 5 cells by changing them to black 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a spaced-out matrix of identical horizontal colored dominoes, where each domino represents one cell of an underlying compact grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a single column with 3 rows.
Describing how to transform the grid: Convert the domino layout into the compact grid of colors, then remove the dominant repeated color. For each remaining color, find the smallest axis-aligned bounding rectangle containing all of its occurrences in the compact grid. Sort the colors by the area of that rectangle from largest to smallest, using occurrence count as a tie-breaker, and write the colors top-to-bottom.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 6x3 grid made of two stacked 3x3 patterns: a brown pattern on top and a blue pattern on bottom.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top 3x3 pattern and bottom 3x3 pattern cell by cell. If both corresponding cells are black, put a red cell in the output. Otherwise, put black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing several separate non-black 2x2 colored blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 10x10 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all original colored blocks unchanged. For each 2x2 colored block, count how many distinct colors appear inside it, then draw a green vertical rectangle directly below that block, with the same 2-column width and with height equal to that distinct-color count.

Describing the input grid: A 24x24 grid has one 5x5 blue occluder hiding cells in a design that is symmetric under horizontal reflection, vertical reflection, and 180-degree rotation.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 5x5.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each cell of the blue block, look at its three symmetric partner cells: left-right mirror, top-bottom mirror, and 180-degree rotated position. Ignore any partner that is also blue, and output the majority color among the remaining partner cells.
